data.title: Japanese Inflation Accelerates, Raising Odds Of Another Rate Hike
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang

Fresh food prices propelled inflation higher in January

Japan's consumer price inflation accelerated to 4.0% year-on-year in January, in line with market consensus (vs 3.6% in December), as fresh food surged 21.9% (vs 17.3% in December). Rice prices skyrocketed 70.9%. Excluding fresh food, core CPI rose faster than expected to 3.2% (vs 3.0% in December, 3.1% market consensus). Costs of eating out have been on the rise for the past three months. Inflation rose 0.5% month-on-month, seasonally-adjusted, with goods up 1.0% and service costs unchanged. We believe that the BoJ is likely to focus on core trends rather than headline inflation. Though in line with BoJ projections, price dynamics support the central bank's rate normalization strategy.

JPY rising as market expectations for a rate hike increase

A variety of factors has market expectations pivoting toward rate hikes: recent hawkish comments from BoJ officials; stronger-than-expected GDP data; and a rising CPI. As such, 10Y Japanese government bond (JGB) yields rose significantly, while the JPY surged against the USD over the past week.

We believe that the BoJ prefers to avoid any sudden moves in market rates, as the currency reaction could dampen economic sentiment and activity. A sudden jump in long-term yields would increase uncertainty about corporate and government funding. Sharp JPY appreciation may hurt earnings. So, the BoJ will try to limit the extremes of market reactions. This morning, Governor Ueda told parliament that the BoJ will keep JGB buying operations flexible. Trading in the JPY and JGBs seemed to calm down somewhat after his remarks.

Flash PMIs advanced in February

Japan's flash purchasing managers indexes showed services are helping to drive the economic recovery, while manufacturing remains sluggish. Overall, both indices improved from the previous month. The service PMI rose to 53.1 in February from 53 in January, marking the fourth consecutive month of expansion. The manufacturing PMI edged up to 48.9 from 48.7, but has remained in contractionary territory for eight months. Output and new orders were up. We are concerned that US tariffs will eventually dampen the economy. But so far, the negative impact hasn't materialised.

Inflation outlook uncertain

Tokyo inflation data, due out next week, is expected to ease modestly in February. Food prices are likely to rise further, but renewed energy subsidy programmes should offset some gains. We also believe the government is likely to introduce measures to stabilise rice prices, which could tame broader food costs. Yet with Trump's tariff policies intensifying, the BoJ will remain quite cautious going forward.

Core inflation is likely to stay above 2% for a considerable time, which allows the terminal rate to reach 1.25% by 2026

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Bancolombia: Electoral Trade, Too Early To Play
data.description:
In my previous analysis of Bancolombia (NYSE:CIB), I rated the stock as a "sell" despite the shares being undervalued. This was due to the net interest margin guidance for 2025, where the bank expected a considerable reduction of 800 bps, particularly due to the expected trajectory of the Colombian benchmark interest rates.

Since November, several factors have changed, and although I had a defendable thesis similar to analysts from JPMorgan and Goldman Sachs, the shares have rallied 33.41% since my rating, and in this analysis, I will update my thesis on Colombia's largest bank.

Q4 Earnings: Credit Conditions Improved Substantially

On Wednesday, Bancolombia surpassed Q4 earnings expectations with an EPS of $1.57, representing a 12.87% difference. In addition, revenue met expectations, obtaining $1.71 billion during Q4 and $6.81 billion in the full fiscal year.

The quarter's highlight was undoubtedly the solid provision performance, which dropped to $930 million in Q4, representing a whopping -46.08% decline from Q3. The rise in provisions was a key risk factor during the previous earnings releases. In Q4, provisions started to normalize with a cost of risk of 1.35% due to reduced deteriorations in all segments, but especially in the consumer and mortgage portfolio and updated releases of macroeconomic variables.

Key changes in macroeconomic variables include a change in the inflation forecast for 2025 from 4.3% to 4.0%, with unmoved GDP growth expectations of 2.6%, and expectations of year-ending policy rates from 6.0% to 6.5% compared to the bank's expectations in Q3. This is due to the central bank's more cautionary approach to reducing rates due to fiscal deficit pressures and a higher-than-expected minimum wage rise.

Loan Portfolio and Net Interest Margin

Bancolombia's loan portfolio grew substantially compared to the growth rates of previous quarters, thanks to a more pleasing credit environment. In Q4, the firm grew its portfolio by 10.0%, with particular strength in commercial and mortgage loans, growing at 11.47% and 15.15% YoY. At the same time, the consumer portfolio grew modestly by 2.26% and reverted the downtrend from declines in previous quarters.

As expected, net interest margins declined 40 bps to 6.4%, but the company improved its year-end NIM forecast from 6.0% to 6.2%. Nonetheless, the portfolio's growth didn't completely counter the decrease of -4.6% in net interest income, but it was countered by a rise in fees of 5.63% and 6.79% in the trading portfolio.

"The net interest margin for the quarter was 6.4%, equivalent to a 42 basis point reduction and 6.8% for the full year, implying a 20 basis points contraction as anticipated, explained by the lower yielding portfolio, partially compensated with the investment portfolio income." Mauricio Wolff, CFO

With these financials, Bancolombia achieved an annualized return on equity of 15.7%, representing a decrease of 40 bps compared to a year ago. Despite this, the company slightly increased its guidance for FY 2025, forecasting a ROE in a range between 14% and 14.5% compared to the previously guided 14%.

Electoral Trade Has Made Colombian Equities Rally

As commented, Bancolombia's shares have rallied substantially since mid-January, with a price return of approximately 34.2%. This rally was helped by several factors, such as the weekend resolution of the tariff conflict between the US and Colombia, a central bank that kept rates unchanged at 9.50% despite expectations for a 25 bps rate cut, and, more importantly, an overweight recommendation from Morgan Stanley analyst, Jorge Kuri, changing his target price on the bank from $40 to $53. Simultaneously, the trading day after the earnings release opened the shares with a positive gap of 3.0%.

This adds to investors' early optimism about a change in government in 2026. Currently, the Colombian president, Gustavo Petro, is accused of multiple acts of corruption. The most recent one was apparently receiving illegal funds to fund his campaign from a smuggler with the alias of "Papa Smurf."

Colombia's politics have been a critical risk factor in investors' confidence, particularly in industries such as oil and gas. Recently, the stock of Ecopetrol (EC) has soared substantially thanks to analysts from JPMorgan and Citi improving outlooks in anticipation of a change of government, and this perspective has been reflected in a positive market sentiment for the overall market.

Although there are still 16 months until the election, polls demonstrate that the president has a poor approval rating of only 29%. Simultaneously, candidates currently supporting the Petro administration (colored in red) mark poorly on the polls.

Valuation: The Shares Remain Undervalued

A day before the earnings call, the company submitted a proposal for a dividend increase to COP 3,900, which implies a 10.3% rise based on a payout ratio of 60% to be paid in one installment, changing the classical timing of a quarterly dividend due to the creation of the new holding company.

With the proposed dividend, Bancolombia has a forward dividend yield of 8.89% on its ADR based on a share price of $43.06 and a dividend of $0.96 multiplied by four, which is the ADR conversion.

As a result, the dividend yield remains attractive based on a historical average of 5.84%, despite the uprise in the stock price, and also compared to the current yield of peers such as Grupo Aval (AVAL) and Credicorp (BAP).

Finally, with an ROE of 15.7%, which is below its discount rate, and a price-to-book of 1.1x, Bancolombia's shares look undervalued. Simultaneously, political factors have caused Colombian equities to be significantly discounted against regional peers, and although the electoral trade is too early, in my opinion, with a change of administration, the sentiment would likely make discounted valuations move closer to fair value.

Takeaway & Stock Rating

Clearly, with these earnings, the bank's backdrop has changed. A softer monetary policy has allowed the bank to increase its NIM expectations, and combined with a growing balance sheet with reduced provisions, the situation now looks better than before.

Although the government seems unable to pass new legislation, Petro still has 16 months in power, which is the enough time to create political uncertainty and further deteriorate diplomatic relationships with the United States.

Considering these earnings, risks, and valuation, I will upgrade my 'Sell' rating of Bancolombia to a 'Hold.'

data.title: Politics And The Markets 02/21/25

This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

data.title: FEPI: A Simple Yet Effective Covered-Call Fund For The Current Market Conditions
data.description:
There are times when adding complexity is not the right move for investors. While some hedge funds like to focus on complex arbitrage and other more creative trading and investing methods, there are also individuals who have consistently beaten the S&P 500 (SPY) and the broader indexes by just allocating capital to specific companies that have outperformed such as (AAPL), NVIDIA (NVDA), or in the past, traditional corporations such as Altria (MO).

With interest rates consistently at near historic lows over the last decade, many investors seeking income and dividends have increasingly turned to new strategies to get regular and substantive income. A new form of investment that has become more commonplace is covered call funds. While financial vehicles mostly started out with basic strategies of owning equity and selling call options, there are a number of new income-focused investments that use a variety of strategies with derivatives, including ones that don't even own stocks.

One covered-call fund that has performed very well with very simple options and investment strategy is the REX FANG & Innovation Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:FEPI). This exchange-traded fund seeks to track the Selective Fang Innovative Index that and is comprised primarily of Nasdaq-100 stocks, but FEPI holds only 15 companies.

FEPI has offered investors total returns of 33.08 percent since the fund's inception in October of 2023, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 42.52 percent during the same time period.

I last wrote about the REX FANG & Innovation Equity Premium Income ETF (FEPI) in April of 2024, and I rated the fund a buy primarily because of the fund's simple but effective options strategy, as well as the tax advantages of this investment. FEPI has offered investors total returns of 15.81 percent since I wrote that article nearly 10 months ago. Today, I am reiterating my buy rating. FEPI's covered-call strategy has been effective at minimizing net asset value losses and enabling the fund to take part in most of the upside of the underlying equities the fund holds. This investment's decision to hold equities also gives this ETF tax advantages over other covered-call funds such as many Yieldmax investments that use synthetic call spreads as a stock replacement strategy. FEPI's income is taxed nearly entirely as the return of capital.

FEPI invests in large-cap tech companies and the ETF's holdings are fairly concentrated. The exchange-traded fund HAS an expense ratio of .65 percent, a trailing yield of 26.62 percent, and 481.52 million in assets under management. The trailing yield is misleading since this fund's regular payments will change based on the monthly income the ETF takes in, which can vary significantly based on a couple of factors, including the volatility premiums in the options being sold. FEPI's options strategy is simple and straight-forward. The fund owns 15 companies and sells call options against holdings that have generally been around 5 percent to 10 percent out-of-the-money.

FEPI invests 67 percent in technology stocks, 21 percent in communication holdings, 11.65 percent in consumer cyclical companies, and 2.52 percent in government bonds. This ETF invests in 15 stocks, and each position is equal weight. FEPI rebalances monthly and reconstitutes quarterly to make sure the composition of the fund stays balanced.

Many of the best-performing covered-call funds such as the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI), use a similar strategy of selling out-of-the-money calls. This approach has minimized losses to the net assets and also enabled investors to take part in more of the upside of the underlying equity positions. FEPI sells out-of-the-money calls against the 15 individual equity positions the fund holds.

While most of the core holdings of this fund have performed very well since the fund's inception in late 2023, FEPI's net asset value is still up an impressive 23.79 percent since that time.

This ETF has also been able to pay both consistent and substantive monthly income since the fund's inception.

FEPI's payments have ranged from a low of nearly $1.08 a share in October of 2024, to a high of nearly $1.19 in March of last year. Since FEPI is selling off some of the upside gains in the underlying equities the fund holds while not protecting downside risks, this ETF is not likely to outperform the broader indexes over an extended period of time. Still, most income investors are willing to give up some gains for more predictable income, as long as the payouts are substantive.

FEPI's current payouts are taxed nearly entirely as return on capital too, which gives this fund a significant advantage over other covered called funds that don't own equities and have these investments payouts taxed at ordinary income levels, as is the case with many of the YieldMax funds that use synthetic call spreads to take long positions.

All investment theses have risks, and the core holdings of FEPI have performed very well in the limited time since the fund came to market in October of 2023. While moderately higher levels of volatility help covered-call funds because the implied volatility premiums in the call options these investments sell are higher, huge moves can create additional risks to the principal since these funds usually sell off some of the upside without maintaining downside protection. FEPI does not protect against downside risks, this fund is not likely going to perform as well moving forward as the fund since this ETF's inception. FEPI's holdings are also more concentrated than most investments, even though the ETF holds mostly less risky large-cap companies, so the fund is more vulnerable to significant moves in any one position.

Investing strategies often have to evolve even as long-term financial objectives remain the same because of changing market conditions. Still, sometimes overcomplicating capital allocation isn't the best approach. While FEPI uses a simple approach to investing, this fund focuses primarily on the top-performing large-cap tech companies while using an options strategy that should be able to consistently offer investors consistent income and some solid total returns as well.

data.title: Hasbro: Growth In EBITDA Expected Realistic Despite Tariff Risk
data.description:
Hasbro (NASDAQ:HAS) is a train we're a bit disappointed we've missed, since it was on the radar for a few months now after hitting local lows around Christmas. Qualitatively, the Universes Beyond sets being released in MTG (often with starter kits to recruit fans from these other properties), while initially seeming a bit gimmicky with cards released from partnered franchises like Assassin's Creed, LotR and Final Fantasy, are actually very playable and don't detract from the game. In addition to that, the IP stable in general, including a lot of Wizards of the Coast IPs, are obviously attractive with Baldur's Gate 3 the most obvious demonstration of that lately, also being an outlier in terms of lifetime revenue, with unexpected blips in player counts even well after release, and selling less than half of the total titles in the first 6-8 months. A very long tail. Longer-term, the higher margin segments are poised to grow, led by WotC and the general focus on licensing out their IPs. This also limits their risk with reference to tariffs, affecting their toys segment, although to a diminished extent as efforts to move production away from China at Hasbro started in 2019, now significantly less exposed than the average toy manufacturer. Indeed, guidance is of EBITDA growth into 2025 including tariff assumptions baked into results, thanks to the cost-cutting and positive mix effects expected with reference to margin and tariff exposure. Markets responded well today, even if the Q4 results were not that fantastic themselves.

Latest Results

Let's go straight to the revenue breakdowns for the Q4 results. WotC leads the product stable by margin. It's at 40% operating margin compared to the 20% group average, and is an important source of positive mix effects whenever this segment grows ahead of the group, which is what is expected into 2025. MTG revenues are down YoY because of a tough comp with the Universes Beyond LotR release last year in the Q4, probably the first really noticed partnered release as part of this new strategy. In general, the company released one less set this year compared to last as well as LotR probably having greater than average draw. They also quite recently released a major revision to 5th edition, also the source of controversy as it was perceived as woke to remove a staple of RPG games, or at least the term referring to, races. In the case of D&D, there is no sign that this has caused a loss in the playerbase, or at least of demand for the manuals, possibly because D&D only in the last 10 years has reached a new, younger audience en masse thanks to D&D livestreams like Critical Role and other factors that have re-introduced the property. We should also not there were aggressive practices in the works that got leaked leading to a major boycott that also affected the box office of the D&D movie, forcing WotC to back off from the OGL proposal. The proposal would have affected people who streamed D&D games and platforms like Roll20, an essential resource which is also free for online D&D play.

On a FY basis WotC revenues were up slightly, on account of a long tail on Baldur's Gate 3 sales, exceeding expectations considerably in the 2024 despite the fiscal year starting 6 months after the game's original release, as well as performance from the various sets released this year.

The digital and licensed gaming revenues continue to grow within WotC. This is in part a testament to the unbelievable continued performance of Baldur's Gate 3. Normally, a game will sell half of its lifetime volumes in the first 6 months from release. Baldur's Gate 3 sold around 15 million copies 8 months after release, and today it's estimated they've sold around 35 million copies just on PC, not including console volumes. This means a longer tail than most games which will sell half of its volumes in the first 6 months. Q4 results show that the moderation in these sales isn't enough to hit the figures. They also had success with their own release of Monopoly Go!, making considerable money on this F2P title.

The non-Hasbro TV and film segment was the segment associated to eOne film, in its stable series like Yellowjackets which were quite successful, but since sold to Lionsgate in its entirety at a significant write-down.

Consumer products was solid, and apparently discounting is on the down and inventories are quite low. In other words, the toy segments is looking pretty good into the coming year. General licensing revenues also from the toy IPs has been up significantly, such as a My Little Pony trading card game and other initiatives. Consumer products was more pressured in the FY results as economic deceleration set in a bit and discretionary spend was tightened, with more discounting also affecting earlier parts of the year compared to into the holiday season.

In terms of the overall results, excluding the eOne Film disposal, revenues were down 3%. WotC was down 7% due to the fewer set releases this year. Operating margin improved 14 points YoY, driven in part by the $227 million annualised cost savings achieved by the company on a net and annual run-rate basis so far. This is about 25% of the total cost savings being targeted by the company into 2027, meaning a significant lift to margins remains possible. Record margins were hit for WotC for the FY.

Bottom Line

The company expects EBITDA to grow to $1.1 billion into the coming year, a 4% growth. This includes, according to management, the effect of the tariffs including those that could come in on China. This is very important to hear because Hasbro produces a very considerable amount of toys in China. They have considerably less exposure compared to other toy manufacturers, where 80% of toy manufacturing overall occurs in China. Hasbro is around or below 50%, and they started this initiative years ago. The issue is that they also probably moved some manufacturing to Mexico, but the guidance includes the announced threats of tariffs on Mexico as well to the extent that their production is occurring there. The WotC business is totally unaffected by the tariff spat from our understanding, and are certainly not exposed to China.

WotC revenues are expected to once again increase ahead of the group, meaning better mix effects at the group level, though the increased releases of Universes Beyond, including major tentpole releases like Final Fantasy, will lower unit economics a little bit because these are from third party licensors - so there are royalty costs associated with these revenue streams.

They are also planning on expanding an IP called Exodus, which they want helmed by a BioWare executive from the "golden age" to try develop that into a valuable and attractive IP. Additionally, they have entered into a deal with Sabre Interactive, which was behind Space Marine II, a well-received and IP-laden game, to develop a new game for Hasbro using one of their main IPs, still to be announced. Altogether, this makes the growth case pretty likely and robust enough to support management expectations for defensible EBITDA growth since we think these efforts are likely to lead to success, in addition to expected growth from WotC which is not a reach, also with momentum of consumer products performing strongly in Q4 trailing into the coming Q1.

60% of Hasbro operating income is from WotC, which is not at risk from tariffs and is also growing. It is IP rich as well. Hasbro trades at around 20x PE adjusted for the loss on disposal. Konami (OTCPK:KONMY), a decent comp for WotC, is at 32x, and Mattel (MAT), a comp for the consumer products business, is at around 11x. A fair blended PE for HAS would be around 24x with these comps, meaning up to 20% upside for HAS at current prices. Not as great as it would be if bought a couple of days ago, but still decent, although with the moderate risks associated with the fact that it is a discretionary consumer product company, though with a rich IP stable and a lot of sources of pricing power and even a vice component associated to the significant profitability associated with selling booster packs within WotC. A buy.

data.title: PepsiCo's Recent Selloff Is A Huge Gift For Long-Term Investors
data.description:
PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP) is one of our favorite consumer staples names on the market.

Why? Because people love to eat.

As the maker of many of today's cheapest, most hyper-palatable foods and beverages, the company has a strong, built-in demand profile from a growing global population.

At the same time, PEP boasts excellent ROE, double digit net profitability, and robust margins on the back of its worldwide distribution and marketing systems.

In the past, we've written about the company from the perspective of investors looking to sell puts on this rock-solid, blue-chip company:

  • Coca-Cola Vs. PepsiCo: Which Is Better Positioned To Sell Puts?
  • PepsiCo: Put Selling Can Yield A Potential 9.2% Return

Historically, we've looked to sell puts - as opposed to owning the stock outright - as we thought that it was the best way to earn cash returns from this slow moving, stable juggernaut.

Now, with the stock's recent selloff, PEP has become - in our view - a truly attractive long-equity play.

Sure, recent earnings were a bit of a disappointment, and shareholders are clearly frustrated by the lack of top-line growth, but we're confident that going forward, PEP's strategy of building and acquiring iconic food and beverage brands - and then leveraging brand pricing power - should re-ignite growth over the long run and produce double-digit annualized returns for the foreseeable future.

Today, we'll dive into the company's recent results, give our outlook for the firm, and explain why we think the recent selloff in PEP shares could be a gift for long-term oriented shareholders.

Sound good? Let's dive in.

PEP's Financials

Starting, as always, with PEP's financials, let's begin by taking a look at the company's recent earnings.

As you can see, over the last few quarters, PEP has been producing some pretty lousy results:

With consistent top line misses and non-existent revenue growth, the firm's growing EPS has largely been powered by increasing gross margins, share buybacks, and improving cost management within the business.

On the bottom line everything appears 'fine', or at least, 'fine-adjacent'. That said, the top line appears to be where the majority of the concern is - at least for now.

Let's dive into some context for these results by taking a quick look at two key charts for PEP.

First, margins. If you recall, PEP's margin story worried investors throughout 2022 and 2023 as the company struggled to navigate a new, high-inflation environment.

While initially good for sales and EPS growth, input prices began to catch up with inflation precisely when stretched consumers were becoming more and more cost-conscious throughout late 2022 and early 2023. The result was a net margin roller coaster, which dented the company's multiple on the open market:

Thankfully, as you can see, PEP has been able to re-build margins over the last few quarters, which has led to the company re-claiming their long-term net margin profile, which stands right around 10%.

This has been achieved through continued optimization of the firm's operations, alongside improving input and end market pricing trends. As inflation has abated, PEP has been able to take advantage of the normalized market.

That said, the punches keep coming. Now that the company is finally out of the woods on the inflation issue, PEP is getting hit with the one-two punch of weight management drugs, like Ozempic and Mounjaro, which are produced by Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly (LLY), respectively.

How has this impacted the business? In short, Recent studies have shown that consumers on these drugs buy materially less calorie dense foods like chips, sweets, and other 'snacky' impulse buys:

Overall, the researchers discovered that the average person who used these medications spent 5.5 percent less at the grocery store during their first six months on the drug. The shift was even starker for people with higher incomes, who spent about 8.6 percent less while food shopping.

The researchers also looked at what these people were spending their money on. They found that that, in general, users bought fewer snack foods, sweets, and other calorie-dense items, as well as so-called "impulse" purchases like chips. Users of these medications were also more likely to consistently buy nutrient-dense foods like yogurt, fresh produce, and nutrition bars.

Clearly, this is a problem for PEP, as a large percentage of the firm's revenues come from FNLA (Frito Lay North America) and QFNA (Quaker Foods North America:

Not only that, but a chunk of the company's non-NA businesses also have revenue derived from many of these food-related products.

Looking more broadly at PEP's financials, the company has struggled to grow revenues given the headwinds presented by pervasive weight loss drug use, as growth rates have fallen:

Now, with YoY top line growth basically hovering at zero, investors have begun throwing in the towel on the stock, with the core fear being that if more people get onto weight loss drugs, that it could have a material impact on PEP's long term business prospects. This makes sense, but we don't think NVO and LLY present an existential risk to PEP's business.

Here's why.

First, on the face of it, right now, roughly 13% of the U.S. population have tried weight loss drugs, and 6% currently use weight loss drugs. This implies two things - that more than nearly a third of obese people in the U.S. have tried weight loss drugs, and that the drugs themselves have a high fall-off rate.

In other words, a solid portion of the end market have tried the drugs, and people who try them don't tend to stay on them due to side effects, personal reasons, high costs etc. Given that PEP's normally slow-growing revenue was only stunted a bit by this development, we don't think there's going to be a material fall-off in PEP product demand from weight loss drugs over the long run, even if those drug costs come down.

Second, and more importantly, PEP's product strategy has already begun to pivot around these developments.

In the future, we expect that better and better weight loss drugs will come into the market, but we also expect that PEP's product portfolio, especially on the food side, will be able to adapt to weight-loss-drug-powered consumer preferences.

Sure, right now, PEP's calorie-dense chips and snack businesses may not be in favor, but the company's long track record of building and buying iconic brands should also continue, which should dynamically put the company at the forefront of consumer food trends, even if those trends get healthier.

We're already seeing this with PEP's recent Siete Foods acquisition, which brought hummus, healthier snack brands, and the "Bare" brand under PEP's umbrella, which we think will appeal more to those seeking healthier, less calorie-dense options:

Finally, global population growth is still projected to rise, which is a final driver for PEP's food and beverage consumption to move higher.

All in all, we see short-term pain for PEP, but as the management continues to execute, we think the company will be able to weather the storm and even thrive, long term.

PEP's Valuation

So - why is the stock's recent selloff such a gift?

Well, as we just said, we think PEP's underlying organic business will do well over the long run, even if growth has slowed for the time being.

This makes the stock's current multiple - at around 20x TTM P/E - appear extremely attractive.

First, on virtually every metric, PEP's stock appears attractive vs. where it has traded historically:

On the top left, you can see PEP's price to sales ratio, which is trading below the long-term standard deviation band (representing an extremely attractive price). Similarly, you can see on the top right, and on the two bottom charts that PEP is trading, variously, in the lower standard deviation band.

Whether you're looking at top line revenues, bottom line profits, cash flows, or PEP's book value, shares appear to be trading at decade-lows, which presents a very interesting buying opportunity.

Not only that, but if PEP can re-ignite top line growth, then we're looking at a very attractive looking valuation for such a scaled, high-quality player over time:

If analysts are right, then with a buy at today's price, you're getting PEP at under 15x 2028 P/E. In today's expensive looking market, we'd take this in a heartbeat.

Not only that, but organic growth isn't the only route to returns here. Considering how low PEP's historical valuation appears, we think the stock could also experience significant multiple expansion, which could drive further appreciation through the end of the decade.

All told, we think PEP's strong underlying prospects and highly attractive price point make the stock a clear 'Strong Buy'.

Risks

That said, there are some risks.

First off, if weight loss drugs have a larger-than-expected impact on snack foods and calorie dense food demand, then PEP's dynamic product line may not be able to keep pace with the rate of change happening on the consumer side.

This could cause market share losses, volume losses, or financial losses that could be hard to come back from, at least in the short run.

Additionally, another bout of financial stress, inflation, or weakening consumer sentiment at a macro level may spell disaster for PEP's share price, even if the company's long-term prospects remain untarnished.

Summary

Overall, though, we see PEP as a high quality, low-risk buy for those seeking double-digit appreciation for the next few years in an otherwise expensive-looking market.

With an attractive multiple, solid brand portfolio, and excellent profitability metrics to boot, we see PEP as a 'Strong Buy' over the interim. Plus, you get a well-backed 3.8% dividend for your cash while you wait.

Stay safe out there!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Digital Turbine: Price Spike Driven By Guidance Increase, But A Small Margin Of Safety (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Digital Turbine, Inc. (NASDAQ:APPS) recently delivered an impressive increase in guidance for the year 2025, which motivated the traders to buy a significant number of shares. Strong international performance seems to be the main driver for the company's EBITDA and FCF expectations in 2025. In my view, the company's transformation, which took place in 2024 and may continue in 2025 and 2026, could lead to a significant decrease in product development expenses. As a result, APPS could enjoy significant free cash flow growth from 2025 to 2035. With all that being said and considering the current stock price, I do see upside potential in the stock price, but the margin of safety decreased substantially in February. My previous article proposed to buy shares at less than $2 per share. Right now, the company trades at around $5 per share.

Digital Turbine

Digital Turbine, Inc. offers a mobile growth platform for advertisers, publishers, and device original equipment manufacturers. With proprietary technology, the company appears to offer substantial support and monetization opportunities for clients that receive operational efficiency benefits.

The numbers in the most recent quarterly report included significant surprises, with better than expected Non-GAAP EPS of $0.13. In addition, the company's quarterly revenue was better than expected, equal to $134 million. With these numbers, the stock price recently increased from less than $2 to touch the $5 mark.

The company increased its fiscal 2025 outlook. 2025 revenue is expected to be between $485 million and $490 million, with non-GAAP adjusted EBITDA between $69 million and $71 million. Thanks to these new numbers, the stock price increased substantially in 2025.

When I first talked about APPS, the company's EV/EBITDA was significantly lower than what we see today. The company traded at close to 8x Non-GAAP FWD earnings, the Ev/ FWD EBITDA was also close to 8x. Right now, APPS trades at close to 13x EBITDA. With this in mind, I decided to review the company's new developments announced in the last quarter.

Decrease In Revenue, And Decreases In Product Development Expenses

In the last quarter, the company noted a decrease in revenue growth driven by a significant decrease in the device volumes. With that, I think the best financial figures are related to the company's operating expenses, not the revenue growth.

The decrease was primarily driven by lower new device volumes, partially offset by an increase in revenue-per-device in the US region and internationally. These decreases in revenue were primarily driven by the Europe, Middle East, and Africa regions and were partially offset by increases in the Asia Pacific and China regions. Source: 10-Q

Performance and brand advertising revenue declined by approximately $1,134 and advertising exchange revenue declined by approximately $23,226, primarily due to weaker demand and the impact of the consolidation and exiting of certain legacy AdColony platforms and business lines. Source: 10-Q

According to the last 10-Q, the company noted decreases in product development expenses, linked to the company's transformation that I discussed in my previous article. I think that we will most likely see further benefits of the company's transformation phase in 2025. In my view, a significant decrease in operating expenses could bring substantial EBITDA growth and FCF from 2025 to 2030. In this regard, the following lines were reported in the last quarter.

The decrease in product development expenses was primarily due to lower employee-related costs, including base salary and incentive compensation of $3,010, lower hosting and software costs of $3,162, reduced third-party development costs of $1,283, and other operating costs, including facilities and travel of $2,369. Source: 10-Q

The Transformation Project Is Expected To Continue In 2025 And 2026

The company recently announced a new HR system, global cloud-based enterprise resource planning, and new cost efficiencies, which could bring further EBITDA growth in 2026. In this regard, it is worth noting that the company expects to continue to report costs associated to deployment phases in 2026. in my view, costs associated to new transformation will most likely decline, which may accelerate FCF and net income growth in the near future.

Beginning in fiscal year 2023, the Company entered into a business transformation project that includes the implementation of a new, global cloud-based enterprise resource planning (“ERP”) system to upgrade our existing enterprise-wide operating systems. Additionally, a new human resource (“HR”) system was also implemented to streamline employee management processes and enhance organizational effectiveness. We are also undertaking the consolidation of existing ancillary systems and deploying other new platforms and systems to improve our operations and drive business and cost efficiencies. Source: 10-Q

Costs are anticipated to be incurred through various deployment phases that are expected to continue through early fiscal year 2026. Source: 10-Q

My Financial Expectations, And Price Target Of $8.69

The company reported TTM free cash flow of $43 million. However, 2023 FCF was not far from $55 million, and 2022 free cash flow stood at $169 million. In addition, the company is expecting an increase in future financials in 2025 driven by the company's international on-device business. Besides, the app market is expected to grow at close to 7.48% from 2022 to 2029.

With the anniversary of three year leases here in the US, new AI features and new flagship device launches, we do expect to see stable device sales in the US in 2025. But the highlight here is our nice breakthrough in our international On-Device business. Source: Earnings Call

Total revenue in the App market is projected to reach US$437.75bn in 2022. Total revenue is expected to show an annual growth rate of 7.48%, resulting in a projected market volume of US$781.70bn by 2029. Source: Statista

With this in mind, I assumed that free cash flow could grow from $83 million in 2025 to $108 million in 2030 and $128 million in 2035. Also considering cost of capital of 8.5%, I obtained NPV of future free cash flow of about $718 million. Now, by adding the net present value of the 2035 terminal value, I obtained total enterprise value of $1.29 billion. Finally, with net debt of $379 million, the company is valued at $8.6 per share. In my view, most analysts out there would be using a cost of debt of about 7.0%-14.2% and cost of equity of 8.0% - 10.8%. With these numbers, cost of capital of 8.5% makes sense to me.

  • WACC: 8.5%
  • 2035 xFCF Multiple: 11x
  • NPV of FCF: $718.20 million
  • NPV of Terminal Value: $572.93 million
  • Enterprise Value: $1,291.14 million
  • Net Debt: $379 million
  • Equity $912.14 million
  • Shares Outstanding: 105 million
  • Target Price: $8.69

Competitors seem to trade at 20x TTM EBITDA, and 16x FWD EBITDA. Besides, peers show a sector median PE Non-GAAP FWD of 25x. APPS trades at 17x Non-GAAP FWD earnings, and 13x FWD EBITDA. Hence, the margin of safety is not that large. I decided to close my position in the stock.

Risks From A Decrease In Mobile Phone Sales Volume

Among all the risks that I foresee, in my view, weak demand for new mobile devices is the most serious. We already saw significant declines in volume in 2024, and the company noted that future weakness in the sale of new mobile devices is likely. As a result, in my opinion, we could see lower revenue growth in the coming years. Even if the company lowers its operating expenses, lower revenue growth may lead to free cash flow growth declines.

We are impacted by declining volume of sales of new mobile devices by our partners. We believe this is driven by the impact of inflation, economic uncertainty, and their potential impacts on consumers. These negative macroeconomic trends have resulted, and may continue to result in, a decrease in mobile phone sales volume. Continued weakness in the sale of new mobile devices is likely to continue to impact our business, financial condition, and results of operations, the full impact of which remains uncertain at this time. Source: 10-Q

Risks From Increases In The Interest Expenses

I the nine months ended December 31, 2024, the company reported a double-digit increase in interest expenses, which many investors, in my view, may dislike.

For the three and nine months ended December 31, 2024, interest expense, net, increased by $780 or 10.2% and $3,028 or 13.2% respectively, compared to the three and nine months ended December 31, 2023. Source: 10-Q

Moreover, if the company receives more debt from debt investors in the future, the net debt/EBITDA may increase. The demand for the stock could decrease, and the share price could fall. In 2024, the company reported $839 million in total assets and long-term debt of $408 million. The total amount of long-term debt increased in 2024.

Risks From Tariffs And International Politics

APPS receives a considerable amount of dollars from the United States, but the company is also present in other jurisdictions. Given the number of new tariffs announced in the United States, I would be expecting a response from other countries in Europe, Canada, China, and other parts of the world. Besides, these territories may also decide to block software from the United States. Under certain conditions, APPS could see a decline in its revenue growth in certain territories outside the United States.

Conclusion: The Upside Potential In The Stock Price Is Limited

Digital Turbine recently reported a large increase in the EBITDA guidance for 2025, which recently resulted in a substantial increase in the stock price. I wrote about the company when the shares traded at less than $2 per share. Right now, the share price is significantly higher than $2 per share. In my view, the transformation project and further costs associated with the implementation of the new enterprise-wide operating system and new human resource system could bring substantial efficiency in 2025 and 2026. Besides, we could see a further decrease in product development expenses, which may also accelerate future EBITDA growth. With all that being said and given the risks with regard to the total amount of debt and potential weak demand for new mobile devices, in my view, the upside potential in the stock price is limited. We may see further increases in the stock price in the coming years because Digital Turbine reports a beautiful business model. However, I decided to close my position in the stock.
